results.possibleIdentity: William Carey Gwin (commonly known as Bill Gwin) is an American painter and video artist who was a prominent figure in the early development of video art during the late 1960s and 1970s. He was an original member of the National Center for Experiments in Television (NCET), an experimental workshop founded in 1967 at the PBS station KQED in San Francisco with support from the Rockefeller Foundation. Gwin is described as one of the most 'fine-arts-oriented' video artists of the period, often balancing his practice between painting and electronic media. His work at NCET involved collaborations with other pioneers like Stephen Beck, Don Hallock, and Warner Jepson, contributing to a series of experimental tapes known as 'Electronic Notebooks.' Among his notable video works are 'Irving Bridge' and 'Sweet Verticality.' He also authored technical and theoretical texts on the medium, such as 'Video Feedback: How to Make it; An Artist's Comments on its Use; A Systems Approach.' Gwin's work was featured in early influential exhibitions of video art, including events organized by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), such as 'Some More Beginnings' (1968) at the Brooklyn Museum. Documentation related to his career is preserved in archives such as the Sorel Etrog fonds at the Art Gallery of Ontario.

results.possibleIdentity: Bernard Childs (1910–1985) was an American painter, printmaker, and sculptor active in New York and Paris. He is best known for pioneering the use of industrial power tools to create "power drypoints" (direct engravings on metal plates) and for his late-career experiments with technology and light. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, Childs expanded his work into three-dimensional "light sculptures"—engraved acrylic sheets (Plexiglas) illuminated from below or within. A 1969 retrospective at the Storm King Art Center in New York marked the first public showing of these illuminated works. His artistic philosophy emphasized the fusion of industrial tools with creative expression, once stating that his interest in metals and machines was the core of his development in "light, electronics and machines." While his niece, the choreographer Lucinda Childs, was a prominent participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) program "9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering," Bernard's own use of modern materials and mechanical tools aligns with the organization's mission of bridging art and engineering. He lived and worked at the Hotel Chelsea in New York and maintained a studio in Paris for many years.

results.possibleIdentity: Gerda Tasgal Kliegman (1908–1996) was an artist based on the North Fork of Long Island, New York. She is recognized by the Southold Historical Museum as part of a significant community of artists who lived, worked, or exhibited in the North Fork region before 1970. Her work and biography are featured in the award-winning publication "A Shared Aesthetic: Artists of Long Island's North Fork" (2008), which documents historical artists of the area. Kliegman was active during the late 1960s and early 1970s, aligning with the period of activity for the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), though her specific role or contributions to E.A.T. projects are not detailed in general search summaries. She was married to Israel Kliegman (1906–1982). A headshot of the artist was preserved and shared via Wikimedia Commons in early 2024, and attempts were made to document her legacy through a Wikipedia entry at that time.

results.possibleIdentity: J. Handel Evans (also known as John Handel Evans) is an architect, academic leader, and artist who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. In 1966, while at the Department of Architecture at the University of Oregon, he corresponded with the light art pioneer Thomas Wilfred regarding his thesis work, which involved the intersection of light, sculpture, and architecture. This correspondence was later published in the journal 'Leonardo' in 1970. Evans is associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, as evidenced by his inclusion in the E.A.T. section of the 'Leonardo' index. His artistic practice has included the creation of handwoven abstract tapestries, and his work is held in several museum collections, including the Museum of Ventura County and the Mingei Museum. Professionally, he had a distinguished career in higher education, serving as the Acting President of San Jose State University and the founding Planning President of California State University Channel Islands (CSUCI), where he also helped establish the Studio Channel Islands art center.

results.possibleIdentity: Donald C. "Don" Little is a prominent American musician and educator primarily known as a tubist and low-brass specialist. He began his professional activity in the late 1960s and early 1970s, earning a Bachelor of Music in Education from the Peabody Institute of Johns Hopkins University in 1970 and a Master of Music from Northwestern University in 1971. He served as the Principal Tuba of the Fort Worth Symphony Orchestra for over 21 years (until 2001) and became a Regents Professor of Tuba at the University of North Texas College of Music. His connection to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement is likely through his involvement in contemporary and experimental music; his chamber ensemble, BL Lacerta, performed and collaborated on works by major E.A.T.-associated artists, including John Cage and Pauline Oliveros, particularly during the 1980s at venues such as the Dallas Museum of Art.

Don Little was a technical professional active in the 1960s who specialized in instrumentation and photography. He served as the head of the instrumentation and photography section at the Suffield Experimental Station in Canada during the mid-1960s. During this time, he was involved in documenting high-level scientific experiments (such as blast wave simulations) that involved collaboration with contractors from Bell Telephone (Bell Labs). Given that Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) was founded by Bell Labs engineers and frequently involved photographers and technicians to document and realize multidisciplinary works, this Don Little fits the profile of a technical artist or collaborator from the period whose work bridged the gap between engineering data and visual media.

results.possibleIdentity: Esther R. Gentil (1918–2011) was a Miami-based artist, teacher, and craftsperson who was active during the late 1960s and 1970s. She is best known for her oil paintings and extensive work in hand-painted fine porcelain and china, including vanity sets, sake sets, and berry bowls, which she typically signed as "Esther Gentil." She lived in Miami, Florida, for much of her life and served as a past President of the Sertoma Club. Her artistic legacy includes works that are still traded as vintage collectibles today, often noted for their delicate floral motifs and gold accents. While she matches the name and active period provided, search results do not explicitly link her to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization.

Esther Gentle (1899–1991), also known as Esther Gentle Rattner, was a prominent American sculptor, painter, and printmaker active in the New York City art scene during the 1960s and 1970s. She founded the Esther Gentle Gallery in Manhattan in 1951 and was the second wife of abstract expressionist Abraham Rattner. Her work included abstract sculptures and original lithographs, some of which were commissioned by Associated American Artists. Gentle is mentioned in historical accounts of the New York avant-garde and Pop Art movements, which frequently overlapped with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) group. She is listed in dictionaries of the period alongside E.A.T. and other mid-century experimental organizations. A major collection of her work is housed at the Leepa-Rattner Museum of Art in Florida.

results.possibleIdentity: Jill Rosenberg was a staff member and administrator for Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a prominent non-profit organization founded in 1966 by Billy Klüver, Fred Waldhauer, Robert Rauschenberg, and Robert Whitman to foster collaborations between the arts and sciences. Active during the late 1960s and early 1970s, she served as the Office Manager and was a key figure in the organization's daily operations at its New York City office (located at 9 East 16th Street). She played a significant role in managing the Technical Services Program, which was E.A.T.'s matching service that connected over 6,000 artist members with engineers and scientists to realize technologically advanced art projects. Her name appears in administrative records, correspondence, and staff bios within the Experiments in Art and Technology archive (housed at the Getty Research Institute). She is also documented as part of the staff during major E.A.T. projects, such as the design and programming of the Pepsi Pavilion for Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, and was involved in the production of the E.A.T. Bulletin and the 1968 exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum.

Jill Nassau Rosenberg is an artist who began her career as a graphic artist and illustrator in the late 1960s. She worked for numerous advertising agencies and commercial companies until 1990, after which she transitioned into fine art, specifically representational oil portraiture and fauvistic landscapes. Based on the timeline of her activity starting in the late 60s, she aligns with the period of high activity for Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), although her primary documented profile emphasizes her work as an illustrator and painter rather than a technological collaborator.

results.possibleIdentity: Dr. Morris Nitsun (1943–2022) was a South African-born artist, psychologist, and psychotherapist who was active in the late 1960s and throughout the 1970s. After moving to the United Kingdom in 1968, he became an internationally renowned consultant psychologist while maintaining a successful career as an artist. He studied art at St Martin's and the Central School of Art in London. His artistic output from the 1970s includes abstract oil paintings and works on paper, which were featured in various solo exhibitions in London and are held in collections worldwide.

Dr. Desmond Morris (b. 1928) is a British zoologist, ethologist, and surrealist painter who achieved significant fame in the late 1960s and 1970s. While best known for his scientific work and books such as 'The Naked Ape' (1967), he has been a prolific surrealist artist since the 1940s, often blending his scientific understanding of animal and human behavior with visual art. His profile as a scientist-artist hybrid aligns with the interdisciplinary goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which promoted collaborations between artists and scientists during the same period.

Robert Morris (1931–2018) was a central figure in American conceptual and minimalist art during the 1960s and 1970s and was significantly associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) circle. He held a PhD in Art History from Hunter College (CUNY), which he earned in 1966. Morris was involved in major E.A.T. related projects and exhibitions, including the '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' (1966) and the 'Some More Beginnings' exhibition at the Brooklyn Museum (1968). His work frequently explored the intersection of physical materials, performance, and technological systems.
